characters.blurb: Caius Marcius, later Coriolanus for Corioli. Proud patrician warrior who could beat Volscians but not court the Roman demos.
Chief of the Volscians who first hated Marcius, then used him against Rome, then had him killed when peace returned.
Led the women’s embassy that shamed Coriolanus into sparing the city—love of mother over revenge.
Went with Volumnia and the matrons to the Volscian camp; her silence and tears weigh beside the mother’s speech.
Commander who witnessed Marcius’s storm of Corioli and bestowed the surname Coriolanus.
City Marcius took almost alone while the main army faltered; from it he wore the name Coriolanus.

characters.relation: Matron who urged the women’s embassy
characters.blurb: Inspired Volumnia and the Roman women to go as suppliants—the civic counterpart to male arms.
Plebeian leader who drove the charge that ended in Coriolanus’s exile when he opposed cheap grain and popular power.
Named with Sicinnius among the tribunes who made Coriolanus’s contempt for the people a capital quarrel.
Commander in the Volscian campaign who shared the day Marcius won his surname.

characters.relation: Volscian capital he used against Rome
characters.blurb: City of Tullus’s power; Coriolanus made it the base of the invasion that brought Rome to its knees.
Italian nation Rome fought for generations; Coriolanus’s exile made their arms nearly fatal to his mother city.
Appears in the early Republic’s class strife that frames Coriolanus’s refusal to flatter the plebs.

characters.relation: God of the Capitol he nearly took
characters.blurb: Rome’s chief god; the city’s prayers and the women’s embassy turn on piety Coriolanus still feared to outrage.
Market officers tangled in the dearth and distributions that inflamed Coriolanus’s contempt for popular relief.
Latin allies and foes form the Italian background to Volscian campaigns that made Marcius famous.

characters.relation: Peaceful king cited in the mourning law
characters.blurb: Numa’s law fixed the longest permitted mourning at ten months; Plutarch cites it when describing Volumnia’s grief and Coriolanus’s widowed mother.

characters.relation: Poet cited on divine and human motivation
characters.blurb: Plutarch quotes Homer to contrast mortal deliberation with divine compulsion—the context is Coriolanus’s rage, which Plutarch insists was chosen, not imposed by gods.

characters.relation: Republican hero whose sister joined the women’s embassy
characters.blurb: Valeria, who organized the matrons’ embassy to Coriolanus, was the sister of Poplicola; Plutarch notes he was already dead by that time.

characters.relation: Roman citizen who reported Juno’s dream
characters.blurb: A quiet man of modest station who twice dreamed that Juno’s games had been profaned; when he finally reported it, the senators ordered the rites renewed.
Last king of Rome, driven out before the Republic; Marcius first earned his oaken crown fighting to repel Tarquin's attempt to reclaim the throne.

characters.relation: Theban general cited on parental joy
characters.blurb: Plutarch pairs him with Marcius as a soldier who counted his parents' delight in his victories — at Leuctra, above all — as the highest happiness of his life.

characters.relation: Tyrant of Syracuse who sent corn
characters.blurb: Sent a gift of grain to famine-struck Rome, giving Marcius the pretext to demand the people renounce their tribunes before any cheap distribution.

characters.relation: Consul who pledged relief to debtors
characters.blurb: Made the senate's promise to ease debt-bondage after the Sabine campaign; when the senate forgot its word, the plebs seceded — setting the stage for all the turmoil Marcius inflamed.

characters.relation: Royal ancestor of the Marcian house
characters.blurb: Grandson of Numa by his daughter and king after Tullus Hostilius; the most celebrated ancestor of the patrician house into which Coriolanus was born.
Of the same Marcian family; twice elected censor, he then persuaded the people to pass a law forbidding anyone to hold that office a second time.

characters.relation: Moralist cited on a soldier's powers
characters.blurb: Plutarch invokes his dictum that a great soldier needs not only strength of hand but voice and look that terrify the enemy — a standard Marcius fully met.
Trojan hero who built Lavinium in Italy; its siege by Marcius struck Romans as an assault on the very cradle of their nation and its guardian gods.
